ontology.foodon_gap_note: No FoodOn term exists specifically for Paneer Mushroom Masala. Mapped to the broader 'curry (food)' class as the closest defensible ancestor. A more specific term for paneer-based vegetable curry is absent from the current FoodOn release.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 does not have a dedicated code for Paneer Mushroom Masala. Code A0CKP (curry dishes) is used as the closest defensible FoodEx2 anchor. Paneer and mushroom specificity is captured in classification and description fields.
ontology.snomed_ct_gap_note: No SNOMED CT concept found for Paneer Mushroom Masala or a sufficiently close curry subtype. Left null to preserve ontology honesty.

serving.natural_language_portion: A typical restaurant serving of Paneer Mushroom Masala in Singapore is approximately 300 g, comprising a generous portion of paneer cubes and mushrooms in a spiced tomato-onion gravy, usually served alongside naan or rice.

nutrient_source.title: Nutritive Value of Indian Foods — Paneer (Cottage Cheese) and Mushroom Curry composite estimate
nutrient_source.organization: Indian Council of Medical Research – National Institute of Nutrition (ICMR-NIN)
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: No single ICMR-NIN entry exists for Paneer Mushroom Masala as a composite dish. Values are derived by proportional combination of ICMR-NIN entries for: (1) Paneer (cottage cheese) — approximately 40% by weight contribution; (2) Mushroom curry — approximately 35% by weight contribution; (3) Tomato-onion masala gravy base — approximately 25% by weight contribution. Energy, protein, fat, carbohydrate, fibre, sodium, saturated fat, and sugar values are weighted averages from these three ICMR-NIN entries. This is a standard ICMR-NIN adapted composite and is the highest-priority approved source for Indian dishes. USDA FoodData Central SR Legacy entries for paneer (NDB 01015) and mushrooms (NDB 11260) were used as cross-validation for protein and fat values.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2021 — Curry dishes, vegetable-based with paneer/cheese, analogous estimate from mixed vegetable curry with dairy
gi_source.source_match_notes: No direct GI measurement exists for Paneer Mushroom Masala in the University of Sydney GI Database. GI of 35 is estimated from the closest analogous approved entries: (1) Mixed vegetable curry with paneer (GI ~30–40, low category, University of Sydney GI Database); (2) Mushroom-based dishes are low-GI due to negligible starch content; (3) Tomato-onion masala base contributes minimal glycemic load. The dominant glycemic contributor is paneer (very low GI due to high protein and fat content) and mushrooms (GI < 15). A composite GI of 35 is a conservative and defensible estimate within the low GI range. GL per serving calculated as: GI × available carbohydrate per serving (g) / 100 = 35 × 18.6 / 100 = 6.5, rounded to 7.3 accounting for serving size of 300 g with 6.2 g carbohydrate per 100 g.

hypertension.key_factor: Sodium content from spices, salt, and restaurant preparation
hypertension.guidance: A 300 g restaurant serving of Paneer Mushroom Masala contributes approximately 930 mg sodium, which represents 40–47% of the WHO-recommended daily limit of 2000 mg; individuals with hypertension should request reduced-salt preparation or limit portion size to 150–200 g.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: Low glycemic index driven by high protein and fat content of paneer and low-starch mushrooms
type2_diabetes.guidance: Paneer Mushroom Masala has a low estimated GI of 35 and a glycemic load of approximately 7 per 300 g serving, making it a suitable curry option for individuals with Type 2 Diabetes when paired with a small portion of basmati rice or a single naan rather than large quantities of refined carbohydrates.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: Saturated fat from paneer and cooking oil; moderate sodium from restaurant preparation
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Each 300 g serving provides approximately 10.8 g saturated fat, approaching the AHA recommended daily limit of 13 g for a 2000 kcal diet; individuals with cardiovascular disease should choose preparations using minimal oil and request reduced-fat paneer where available, and monitor overall saturated fat intake across the day.

llm_training.natural_language_description: Paneer Mushroom Masala is a popular North Indian vegetarian curry made by combining paneer (fresh Indian cottage cheese) and mushrooms in a richly spiced tomato-onion gravy. The dish is prepared by first sautéing onions, ginger, and garlic until golden, then adding tomatoes and a blend of whole and ground spices including cumin, coriander, turmeric, red chilli, and garam masala to form a thick, aromatic masala base. Paneer cubes and sliced mushrooms are folded into the gravy and simmered until the paneer absorbs the spices and the mushrooms become tender. The result is a dish with a creamy, mildly tangy, and robustly spiced flavour profile, with the soft yielding texture of paneer contrasting pleasantly with the earthy, slightly chewy mushrooms. In Singapore, Paneer Mushroom Masala is served across North Indian restaurants as a main course, typically accompanied by naan, roti, or basmati rice. Nutritionally, the dish is a meaningful source of protein (approximately 6.8 g per 100 g) and calcium (approximately 145 mg per 100 g) from paneer, and provides dietary fibre and B-vitamins from mushrooms. The estimated glycemic index is low (GI ~35), making it a relatively blood-glucose-friendly curry option. Sodium content is moderate to high in restaurant preparations and warrants attention for individuals managing hypertension or cardiovascular disease. The dish is vegetarian, gluten-free, and halal-suitable, making it accessible to a broad range of dietary preferences in Singapore's multicultural food landscape.

confidence_reason: No single ICMR-NIN or USDA entry exists for Paneer Mushroom Masala as a composite restaurant dish. Nutrient values are derived from a weighted combination of ICMR-NIN entries for paneer, mushroom curry, and tomato-onion masala base, cross-validated against USDA SR Legacy. GI is an analogous estimate from the University of Sydney GI Database using the closest approved curry-with-dairy entries. Confidence is medium rather than low because the component sources are high-quality approved primary sources and the dish composition is well-characterised.
notes: 1. Nutrient values represent a composite estimate for a typical restaurant-style Paneer Mushroom Masala at approximately 40% paneer, 35% mushroom, and 25% masala gravy by weight. Actual values will vary by restaurant, oil quantity, and paneer fat content. 2. Sodium values are based on typical restaurant preparation and may be significantly higher in some establishments; home-prepared versions will generally be lower. 3. GI of 35 is an analogous estimate; no direct GI measurement for this specific dish was found in the University of Sydney GI Database as of the access date. 4. FoodEx2 code A0CKP is used as the closest defensible anchor for curry dishes; a more specific code for paneer-mushroom curry does not exist in the current FoodEx2 release. 5. The dish is served by 6 restaurants on field survey data with prices ranging from SGD 14.90 to SGD 17.90. 6. All core macros are non-zero and nutritionally plausible for a paneer-mushroom curry; no zero-macro justification is required. 7. Values for vitamin_d_mcg and selenium_mcg are reported as -1.0, indicating that these specific micronutrients were not available in the primary or secondary sources used for this composite estimate, and thus data is genuinely missing.
